choices: Cancel one of the variables by adding/subtracting equations
hints: The goal is to reduce to a one-variable equation.
We add or subtract the equations to cancel a variable.
questions.explanation: In the elimination method, we add or subtract the equations to *eliminate* (cancel) one of the two variables, leaving a single equation in the other.

questions.promptTex: \text{Solve by elimination:} \\ x + y = 7 \\ x - y = 3
hints: Add the two equations — $y$ cancels.
$2x = 10 \Rightarrow x = 5$. Then $y = ?$
questions.explanation: Adding: $2x = 10$, so $x = 5$. Substitute into the first: $5 + y = 7 \Rightarrow y = 2$. Solution: $(5, 2)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Given:} \\ 2x + 3y = 13 \\ x + 2y = 8 \\ \text{To eliminate } x, \text{ multiply the second equation by:}
hints: First equation has $2x$; we want $-2x$ in the second so they cancel.
Multiply $x + 2y = 8$ by $-2$ to get $-2x - 4y = -16$.
questions.explanation: Multiplying the second equation by $-2$ gives $-2x - 4y = -16$. Adding it to the first cancels $x$.

hints: Add the equations — the $y$ terms cancel.
questions.explanation: Adding the two equations: $5x = 15 \Rightarrow x = 3$. (And substituting back gives $y = 2$.)

hints: $0 = 0$ is always true — it tells you nothing went wrong, but also nothing constrained.
Both equations describe the same line.
questions.explanation: $0 = 0$ is an identity — always true. The two equations represent the same line, so every point on the line satisfies both → **infinitely many solutions**.

questions.promptTex: \text{Solve by elimination:} \\ x + y = 9 \\ x - y = 3
hints: Add the two equations to eliminate $y$: $2x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 6$.
steps: Add the equations to cancel $y$: $$(x + y) + (x - y) = 9 + 3 \Rightarrow 2x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 6$$

hints: The two equations share the same $x$-coefficient. Subtract one from the other to eliminate $x$.
$(2x + 3y) - (2x - y) = 13 - 1 \Rightarrow 4y = 12 \Rightarrow y = 3$. Then $2x - 3 = 1 \Rightarrow x = 2$.
questions.explanation: Both equations have $2x$ — subtract the second from the first to eliminate it: $4y = 12 \Rightarrow y = 3$. Substitute back into either equation: $2x - 3 = 1 \Rightarrow x = 2$. So $(x, y) = (2, 3)$.
steps: **Recognize the setup.** Both equations have the same $x$-coefficient ($2$). Subtracting one from the other will eliminate $x$ in a single step — no scaling needed.
**Back-substitute.** Use the simpler second equation: $2x - 3 = 1 \Rightarrow 2x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 2$.

hints: Add the two equations: $4x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 3$.
Then $x - 2y = 0 \Rightarrow 2y = 3 \Rightarrow y = 1.5$.

questions.promptTex: \text{To eliminate } x \text{ in:} \\ 2x + 3y = 7 \\ 5x - y = 4 \\ \text{multiply first by} \_\_\_ \text{ and second by} \_\_\_.
hints: Want $x$ coefficients to match (or be opposite). $\text{lcm}(2, 5) = 10$.
Multiply first by 5, second by 2: both have $10x$. Then subtract.
questions.explanation: Multiply first by 5 ($10x + 15y = 35$) and second by 2 ($10x - 2y = 8$). Subtract to eliminate $x$.
steps: Goal: make the $x$ coefficients equal (or opposite).

hints: Multiply second by 3: $6x - 3y = 12$. Add to first: $10x = 30 \Rightarrow x = 3$.
questions.explanation: Multiply second by 3 and add: $10x = 30 \Rightarrow x = 3$, $y = 2$.
steps: Multiply the second equation by 3 (to make the $y$ coefficient $-3$, opposite of the first's $+3$): $$6x - 3y = 12$$

questions.promptTex: \text{If you add two equations and get } 0 = 0, \text{ the system has:}
hints: $0 = 0$ is always true → the equations are dependent.
Same line, infinite solutions.
questions.explanation: $0 = 0$ means the two equations are equivalent (same line) → infinitely many solutions.
steps: When elimination gives $0 = 0$, both equations describe the same line.
Every point on that line satisfies both → **infinitely many solutions**.

questions.promptTex: \text{If you add two equations and get } 0 = 5, \text{ the system has:}
hints: $0 = 5$ is always false → contradiction.
No solution.
questions.explanation: $0 = 5$ is never true → the system has no solution (parallel lines).
steps: When elimination gives $0 = 5$ (or any false statement), the equations are inconsistent.
Geometrically: parallel lines that never intersect → **no solution**.
